coredata.dc:title: Cellulose-based yellow-green emitting carbon dots with large Stokes shift as effective “turn off-on” fluorescence platforms for Cr (VI) and AA dual efficacy detection

coredata.dc:description:
Background Hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) is highly carcinogenic to humans. Ascorbic acid (AA) deficiency can be hazardous to health. And the dual-effect fluorescence detection of them is an important research topic. Carbon dots (CDs) based on cellulose are excellent candidates for the fluorescence probes due to their low cost and environmental friendliness. But most of them exhibit shortwave emission, small Stokes shift and poor fluorescence performance, all of which limit their use. Therefore, there is an urgent need for cellulose CDs with longer emission wavelengths and larger Stokes shifts in dual-effect fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) and AA.
Results Under optimal conditions (180 °C, 12 h), we prepared cellulose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by a simple one-step hydrothermal process, which display longer emission wavelengths (ex: 370 nm, em: 510 nm), larger Stokes shifts (140 nm) and high fluorescence quantum yield (QY: 19.27 %). The continuous “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” fluorescence detection platforms were constructed based on the internal filtering effect (IFE) between Cr6+ and N-CDs, and Cr6+ reduced to Cr3+ by AA at pH = 6. The platform has been successfully simultaneous detect Cr (VI) and AA with a wide range of 0.01–40 μM and 0.1–100 μM. And the lowest limits of detection (LOD) are 0.0303 μM and 0.072 μM, respectively. In the presence of some other metals, non-metal ions and water-soluble acids in the fruits, this fluorescent platform can demonstrate a high level of interference immunity.
Significance and novelty This represents the first yellow-green cellulose-based N-CDs with large Stokes shift for dual-effect detection of Cr (VI) and AA in real water samples and fresh fruits. The fluorescence detection platform has the advantage of low volume detection. Less than 2 mL of sample is required for testing and results are available in <5 min. This method is rare and supply a novel idea for the quantitative monitoring of Cr (VI) and AA.

head.ce:title: Cellulose-based yellow-green emitting carbon dots with large Stokes shift as effective “turn off-on” fluorescence platforms for Cr (VI) and AA dual efficacy detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) is highly carcinogenic to humans. Ascorbic acid (AA) deficiency can be hazardous to health. And the dual-effect fluorescence detection of them is an important research topic. Carbon dots (CDs) based on cellulose are excellent candidates for the fluorescence probes due to their low cost and environmental friendliness. But most of them exhibit shortwave emission, small Stokes shift and poor fluorescence performance, all of which limit their use. Therefore, there is an urgent need for cellulose CDs with longer emission wavelengths and larger Stokes shifts in dual-effect fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) and AA.

Under optimal conditions (180 °C, 12 h), we prepared cellulose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by a simple one-step hydrothermal process, which display longer emission wavelengths (ex: 370 nm, em: 510 nm), larger Stokes shifts (140 nm) and high fluorescence quantum yield (QY: 19.27 %). The continuous “turn-off” and “turn-off-on” fluorescence detection platforms were constructed based on the internal filtering effect (IFE) between Cr6+ and N-CDs, and Cr6+ reduced to Cr3+ by AA at pH = 6. The platform has been successfully simultaneous detect Cr (VI) and AA with a wide range of 0.01–40 μM and 0.1–100 μM. And the lowest limits of detection (LOD) are 0.0303 μM and 0.072 μM, respectively. In the presence of some other metals, non-metal ions and water-soluble acids in the fruits, this fluorescent platform can demonstrate a high level of interference immunity.

This represents the first yellow-green cellulose-based N-CDs with large Stokes shift for dual-effect detection of Cr (VI) and AA in real water samples and fresh fruits. The fluorescence detection platform has the advantage of low volume detection. Less than 2 mL of sample is required for testing and results are available in <5 min. This method is rare and supply a novel idea for the quantitative monitoring of Cr (VI) and AA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The main oxidation states of chromium ions are Cr (VI) and Cr (III) [1,2]. And one of the most frequent heavy elements in industrial effluent is Cr (VI) [3,4], which is toxic and highly carcinogenic to humans. The industrial wastewater discharge standards in many countries strictly limit the content of Cr (VI) to the micromolar level (<0.17 μM) [4,5]. However, Cr (III) is poor fluidity, less toxicity, and harmless. It's essential to detect the Cr (VI) and convert it to Cr (III) in water environments.

As a reductant ascorbic acid (AA) can reduce Cr (VI) to Cr (III) in industrial wastewater. Moreover, AA is necessary for the growth and repair of body tissues [6,7]. Lack of AA intake can pose a great threat to human health and cause adverse effects such as dry and cracked skin, slow wound healing, anemia and decreased resistance, and even life threatening in severe cases [8]. Therefore, it is necessary for human health to design an analytical method with a high sensitivity and rapid response to AA in food to ensure adequate daily intake.

Many attempts have been made to accurately detect AA or Cr (VI), such as electrochemical methods [9], atomic absorption spectrometry [10,11] and spectrophotometric methods [12]. But the practical application of these assays in various fields is hampered because of the disadvantages such as the complex sample pre-treatment, the toxic chemicals in the assay process and detecting only a single substance [13]. The fluorescence probe method, however, offers benefits of quick reaction, low interference, and high sensitivity, which may satisfy the demands of a various substance detection applications in industry [14,15]. Carbon dots (CDs) are innovative and environmentally benign fluorescence nanomaterials with particle sizes below 10 nm and have been the subject of intense research due to their fluorescence features [16]. Cellulose is abundant, cheap and environmentally friendly, and used to prepare CDs. Some literatures reported the preparation of blue fluorescence CDs with different cellulose materials, and the sensors were constructed based on CDs. Due to Cr (VI) has a broad absorption from 200 nm to 500 nm, which supply a chance to quench the fluorescence of CDs based on internal filtering effect (IFE). The sensors detect Cr (VI) by the IFE, and then the fluorescence will be restored because Cr6+ was reduced to Cr3+ by AA, So both Cr6+ and AA may be detected quantitatively using the approach [17–19]. However, most of the CDs prepared by these detection methods emit blue fluorescence. Moreover, their Stokes shift is small (<100 nm), which may cause reabsorption and reduce the fluorescence. At the same time, the CDs with longer emitting wavelengths are better resistant to photobleaching and the interference from background light. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare cellulose-based CDs with larger Stokes shifts (>100 nm) and longer emitting wavelengths.

In this work, p-phenylenediamine was doped to extend the emission wavelength and enlarger Stokes shift. In addition, the synthetic process (Scheme 1 a) and raw material ratios were carefully studied to prepare CDs with optimal fluorescence properties to increase the IFE efficiency. So a “turn-off” fluorescence platform is built to detect Cr (VI). Considering Cr (VI) has a broad absorption from 200 nm to 500 nm, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs can be optimized by the synthetic process and raw material ratios, which will be benefit to increase the IFE efficiency between N-CDs and Cr (VI), and built a “turn-off” fluorescence platform for the detection of Cr (VI). As we all know, AA is nutritious and a reducing substance, and can convert Cr (VI) to Cr (III), then it will interrupt the IFE effect between Cr (VI) and N-CDs to restore the fluorescence, and the recuperative degree of the fluorescence will be enhanced with the increase of the concentration of AA, so N-CDs@Cr (VI) can be used as a “turn-off-on” fluorescence detection platform of AA. At the same time, it is benefited to improve water quality because of the decline of Cr (VI). Experimental proof, the fluorescence detection platform of N-CDs@Cr (VI) can be used more directly for the detection of Cr (VI) in human production effluents and AA in real samples. So far, this kind of fluorescence detection platforms are rare. The ingenious ideas responded well to the concept of sustainable development and “green” environment and showed in Scheme 1b.

N-CDs were created by one-step hydrothermal process utilizing the cellulose acetate (CA) as the base carbon source and p-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source. Briefly, cellulose acetate (1 g, 0.033 mmol L−1) and p-phenylenediamine (0.7 g, 6.473 mmol L−1) were dispersed in 30 mL of ultrapure water, then sonicated for 40 min to make them dispersed well. Subsequently, the mixed solution was put into a Teflon-lined (100 mL) autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C, then cooled to room temperature, and removed and sonicated for 20 min. The obtained solution was subsequently centrifuged three times at 9000 rpm min−1 for 10 min each time to get rid of the precipitate. Then, 0.22 μm filter membrane was used to get rid of big particles, and a dark brown solution was acquired, then the dialysis was performed with ultrapure water at room temperature for 72 h (dialysis bag: Mw = 1000 Da). In the end, the resulting N-CDs were freeze-dried to produce powder under vacuum, which was then kept at 4 °C for future usage.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was calculated using coumarin 307 as the standard by the reference method. The QY of N-CDs was determined utilizing coumarin 307 (QY = 0.56) dissolved in ethanol as a standard according to the following equation [20]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Cr (VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Because Cr6+ exists in the form of HCrO4 − and CrO4 2− ions at pH = 6, it is more comprehensive to detect Cr6+ in a buffer solution. PBS solution has the advantages of non-toxicity, simplicity and pH stability (pH = 6–8), so it was used during measuring UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The detail process was described below. The first step involved dissolving a certain amount of N-CDs into a PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH = 6). Following the addition of various Cr (VI) concentration, the mixture's volume was adjusted with PBS buffer (pH = 6, 0.01 M) to 2 mL. The ultimate concentration of N-CDs in the combined solution was 0.3 mg/mL, and unless otherwise specified, this concentration was employed in subsequent experiments. At the excitation (Ex) wavelength of 370 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after 4 min of incubation at room temperature (25 °C) in the dark. In this way, the efficiency of fluorescence quenching was explored to construct ideal N-CDs and N-CDs@Cr (VI) fluorescence platforms, which can detect Cr (VI) and AA respectively.

Subsequently, various doses of AA were put into 2 mL of N-CDs@Cr (VI) (N-CDs: 0.3 mg mL−1, Cr (VI): 90 μM), which was incubated for 4 min in the dark at room temperature (25 °C), then the fluorescence emission spectra (Ex: 370 nm, Em: 510 nm) were recorded. Substitution of AA and Cr (VI) by the addition of other metal/non-metal ions and other reducing analogues that may be contained in the fruit to test the selectivity of Cr (VI) and AA using the corresponding fluorescence platforms.

The real water environment for Cr (VI) detection were collected from actual river water and lake water for spiked recovery. River water samples were taken from Yufu River in Changqing District, Jinan City, Shandong Province, and the lake water samples were collected from Daming Lake, Jinan City, Shandong Province. The collected real water samples were placed in a centrifuge tube to remove large particles of impurities by centrifugation, and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter, then added with Cr (VI) of various concentrations and mixed with N-CDs (0.3 mg mL−1). The fluorescence spectra of the samples (Ex: 370 nm, Em: 510 nm) were recorded after incubating for 4 min in dark at room temperature. The fresh oranges used for AA testing in the actual samples were sourced from the local supermarket in Jinan, China. Based on the previous literature, the fresh fruit was treated [21]. The fresh oranges were firstly washed thoroughly with ultrapure water and then cut into pieces for the juice extraction process. Centrifuging the juice at 5000 rpm min−1 for 20 min, then the large particles of impurities were removed. After completion of centrifugation, the orange juice was passed through 0.22 μm filter membrane, then diluted 50 times using ultrapure water to the appropriate concentration within the assay range. The samples containing different concentrations of AA were put into lake and river water containing actual sample of Cr (VI). Composition 2 mL of N-CDs@Cr (VI) assay platform (N-CDs: 0.3 mg mL−1; Cr (VI): 35 μM) was reacted for 4 min at room temperature, and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 510 nm. Lastly, the spiked recovery tests were operated in order to determine the content of Cr (VI) and AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimized synthetic process and characterization of N-CDs
The optimized synthetic process of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For getting N-CDs with the best fluorescence intensity, the synthetic process of N-CDs was optimized. Because cellulose acetate served as substrate carbon source and p-phenylenediamine as nitrogen source during the one-step hydrothermal process, the amount of p-phenylenediamine added, as well as reaction time and temperature, may be importantly affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs [22,23]. So it was investigated with the addition of p-phenylenediamine (0.4 g–1.0 g) based on 1 g of cellulose acetate. As depicted in Fig. S1a, the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N-CDs increased firstly and then decreased with the addition of p-phenylenediamine. The highest fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was observed when 0.7 g and 0.9 g of p-phenylenediamine were put into, respectively. So 0.7 g of p-phenylenediamine was selected as the optimal addition amount considering for environmental friendliness and cost saving reasons. Then the reaction temperature (160 °C–210 °C) was optimized at the mass ratio (1: 0.7) of cellulose acetate and p-phenylenediamine, and the N-CDs' fluorescence intensity rose firstly before progressively flattening out with the enhanced reaction temperature (Fig. S1b). The value at the reaction temperature of 180 °C was the highest, which may be that the stable structure of carbon dots could not be formed at lower temperatures. However, the structure and size of carbon dots were the most stable and homogeneous at 180 °C in comparison to those under other reaction temperatures, and after the temperature exceeded 180 °C, and fluorescence intensity is the strongest, so the reaction temperature of 180 °C was selected [24]. Finally, during the synthesis of N-CDs, the impact about reaction time on fluorescence intensity was investigated. Under the above optimal condition of the mass ratio of raw material (CA: p-PD = 1: 0.7) and the reaction temperature (180 °C), the reaction time was explored from 6 to 16 h (Fig. S1c). With increasing reaction time, fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N-CDs displayed the trend of increasing firstly and then decreasing, the fluorescence reached the best at the reaction temperature of 12 h. The possible reason is that the too short or too long reaction time can't form a large number of uniform size CDs, which in turn causes the decrease of fluorescence intensity [24]. Therefore, the optimal preparation conditions of N-CDs in subsequent experiments are the mass ratio 1: 0.7 of cellulose acetate to p-phenylenediamine, reaction time of 12 h at 180 °C.

The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by XPS, FT-IR, XRD and TEM. The TEM image in Fig. 1 (a) and the particle size distribution Fig. 1 (b) show the N-CDs with regular monodisperse morphology between 2 and 6.5 nm, with a 3.47 nm average size. The high-resolution TEM shows that most N-CDs have a distinct lattice structure. The lattice spacing is 0.31 nm, and it's related to the substrate spacing of the lattice plane of graphene (100) [25]. In Fig. 1c, the FT-IR spectra showed that broad absorption peak centered at 3448 cm−1 and 3122 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of the O–H and N–H bonds. The absorption peaks at 1264 cm−1 and 818 cm−1 correspond to the C–H bond stretching vibration on the benzene ring (Ar–H), while the absorption peaks at 1516 cm−1 also prove the existence of benzene ring in N-CDs. Stretching vibration of –COOH on the surface of N-CDs caused the peak of 1380 cm−1 [26,27]. Then, by FT-IR tests on the two precursors, we found that the characteristic absorption peaks in cellulose acetate as well as in p-phenylenediamine are well reflected in the N-CDs. In the 1HNMR (400 Hz) spectra of cellulose acetate (DMSO) in Fig. S2a and N-CDs (D2O) in Fig. S2b, we could see that chemical shifts δ of 1.2 (-CH3) and 1.9 (-CH) for cellulose acetate weren't found in Fig. S2b. Obvious peak of hydroxyl group (δ = 3–4 ppm, –OH) was found in Fig. S2b. These data indicated that the N-CDs were successful synthesized. And it is consistent with that of FTIR spectra. Therefore, the results indicated that the N-CDs were successful synthesized. The XRD spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1d) shows a diffraction broad peak centered at 2θ = 23.1°, and the interlayer spacing is 3.10 Å. This may be related to the graphite's (002) lattice space, a characteristic of graphitic carbon materials [28]. Thus, the above results further demonstrate that N-CDs have a graphite-like structure.

The surface element analysis of N-CDs was further finished utilizing XPS. As seen in Fig. 2 a, at 285.0, 399.7 and 532 eV, full-scan XPS spectrum has prominent peaks, which belong to C1s, N1s and O1s. And these data strongly proved that N-CDs are mostly make of C, N and O. The high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 2b) show three distinct kinds of surface components, which correspond to C–C/CC with a binding energy of 284.5 eV, C–N/C–O with a binding energy of 285.6 eV and CO/CN with a binding energy of 288.0 eV. For the spectra of N1 s (Fig. 2c), the peaks at 399.1 eV, 399.8 eV and 400.4 eV correspond to pyridine N, pyrrole N and N–H, respectively [29]. In addition, the high-resolution spectra of O1s (Fig. 2d) were divided into three peaks at 531.2 eV, 532.3 eV and 533.4 eV, which belong to the CO bond, –OH/C–O bond and O–C–O bond, respectively [30]. In summary, the surface groups of N-CDs by XPS corresponded well with those determined by FT-IR measurements, indicating that N-CDs contains ample hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and amine (-NH2), which can enhance the solubility of N-CDs in aqueous solutions, and likewise laid the foundation for the subsequent detection of Cr (VI) and AA.

Next, the formation mechanism of N-CDs was detailly discussed. The thermal decomposition of cellulose acetate in a hydrothermal reaction involves two major steps: One is that the formation of tar and char, and another is that the production of light gases, including CO, CO2, CH4 and H2O [31]. As shown in Fig. 3 , small molecule products such as levoglucosan are further dehydrated to form different anhydrous sugars [32], and they further react to form a variety of substances/molecules (e.g., ketones, acids, etc.), which collide with other gas molecules coming from the pyrolysis of cellulose, aggregate, and carbonize to form CDs. Lower dissociation energy of CO during carbonization, whereas the amine has a higher dissociation energy, and the amino group remains attached to the carbon skeleton during pyrolysis at 180 °C [33]. Therefore, the added p-phenylenediamine, due to the presence of benzene ring and amines, will directly form a conjugated carbon nucleus during the aggregation carbonization process, and there is no segregation of –NH2 during the pyrolysis process at 180 °C, which can bind to the carbon chain and integrate into the CDs, introducing nitrogen doping, resulting in the formation of N-CDs.

The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were used to gauge the optical characteristics of N-CDs. In Fig. 4 a, the absorption peak at 249 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of CC/NN [34]. The n–π∗transition of CO/C–NH2 on the surface of N-CDs lead to faint shoulder peak at 320 nm, which supplies an ability to emit at longer wavelengths [35]. After comparing the chromaticity diagrams (Fig. 4b), we find that the chromaticity coordinates of N-CDs are (0.123, 0.526), and N-CDs shows obvious yellow-green fluorescence (Fig. 4b) under 365 nm UV light. N-CDs exhibit the maximum fluorescence emission intensity at 510 nm by the excited wavelength of 370 nm in Fig. 4c. Its stokes shift is 140 nm, which is larger in comparison to those reported in other literatures [17,18,29]. The fluorescence platform with a larger Stokes shift can avoid re-absorption and improve the fluorescence performance of the materials, so it has a greater advantage in chemical detection and other applications. As can be seen in Fig. 4c, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 340 nm to 370 nm, the emission peak position of N-CDs was red-shifted. However, when the excitation wavelength was from 370 nm to 420 nm, and the emission peak position of N-CDs mostly remained unchanged, but the fluorescence intensity was significantly decreased (Fig. 4c). This suggests that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs has a certain dependence on the excitation wavelength, which may be related to various surface states and size distribution created by different functional groups in N-CDs. N-CDs exhibit typical excitation-dependent emission behavior, which may be due to the fact that the surface of N-CDs contains different surface defects and functional groups [36]. In addition, some reports have also attributed the red-shift of fluorescence emission to quantum size effects of CDs [37,38]. The fluorescence emission wavelength of N-CDs is gradually red-shifted with increasing particle size. In Fig. 1a and b, it can be seen that the size of CDs varies from 2 to 6.5 nm, and the larger size of CDs may be one of the reasons for the red-shift of fluorescence emission [38]. The FT-IR and XPS results further confirmed that the N-CDs have different surface functional groups (-OH, –COOH, –NH2 et al.), which lead CDs to have different surface state energy and emission traps, in turn exhibit a gradual red-shift of the emission wavelength with different excitations. Some reports have also shown that the introduction CN and –COOH functional groups could reduce the energy level between HOMO and LUMO bandgap and lead the emission wavelength to red-shift [39,40]. The fluorescence QY data were got from Fig. S3, and it was calculated to be 19.27 % according to the equation (section 2.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH effect and photostability of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect was explored in the range of pH 1.5–12.5, and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs raised before pH = 2, then slightly reduced until pH = 10, since then significantly declined (Fig. S4a). This reason may be that N-CDs contain nitrogen atoms on their surface, and in the presence of hydrogen ions under acid solutions, lone pairs of electrons on nitrogen atoms can constitute coordination bonds. Therefore, we hypothesize to conclude that the combination of hydrogen with nitrogen atoms (pyrrole-like N, amino N, and pyridine-like N) and changes in structure, dispersion, and homogeneity of the N-CDs (Fig. S5) lead to lower fluorescence intensities at high pH [41]. This is the cause of the sharp decline in fluorescence intensity when pH > 10.5. But the ability to maintain high fluorescence stability throughout a large pH range (pH = 4.5–10.5) also provides a broader basis for our assay. Furthermore, the N-CDs has remarkable stability in environments with high salt concentrations. As seen in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence intensity of N-CD did not change significantly even at a high concentration of NaCl (1 M). This property has great meaning to practical application of N-CDs under physical salt conditions [16,17]. Furthermore, to assess photostability of N-CDs, we continuously irradiated for 60 min at 365 nm and then measured the fluorescence of N-CDs, and it is mostly unaltered in Fig. S4c, which indicated that they were stable in 60 min. Finally, the photobleaching performance of N-CDs was tested, as displayed in Fig. S4d, the fluorescence intensity could be kept for at least one week without significant change. Overall, the N-CDs is stable with the better fluorescence at a wider pH range, and making it potentially more useful for biological applications and the detection of metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influencing factors during the detection of Cr (VI) by N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensitivity of the assay is highly dependent on experimental parameters, including the incubation temperature and time, pH of the solution, concentration of N-CDs, which are assessed using fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF) (ΔF=F–F
0 , F and F
0 are the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with and without of Cr (VI)) at 510 nm). In Fig. 5 , the related outcomes are displayed. All other parameters were chosen using the control variable method in order to determine each parameter. To achieve sensitive detection of Cr (VI), the pH effect on the fluorescence of N-CDs was previously investigated, the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is stable in the pH range of 4.5–10.5. Because Cr6+ coexists as both HCrO4 − and CrO4 2− in the pH range of 5∼6 [17], we chose to detect the fluorescence intensity in a solution environment at pH = 6 (0.3 mg mL−1). To ensure the constant of pH in the detection system, the solutions were dissolved in PBS buffer (pH = 6, 0.01 M). In addition, the concentration effect of N-CDs in the system for the reaction of N-CDs and Cr (VI) (Fig. 5a) as well as the incubation temperature (Fig. 5b) and incubation time (Fig. 5c) of N-CDs and Cr (VI) on the detection sensitivity was explored.

The different concentration effect of N-CDs on the fluorescence intensity of the system was investigated as seen in Fig. 5a, fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF) was the minimum when the concentration was 0.3 mg mL−1. Therefore, N-CDs solution of 0.3 mg mL−1 was selected as the best concentration for the detection of Cr (VI). The impact of incubation temperatures between 15 and 65 °C on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with best concentration is also shown in Fig. 5b. We could find that the system's fluorescence intensity was quenched as incubation temperature rise and then gradually flattens out, and the quenching effect of the system does not change significantly after 25 °C. Therefore, in order to make the test conditions closer to the practical application, room temperature was chosen for the whole experimental procedure. Moreover, Fig. 5c illustrates the effect of incubation duration on the detection of Cr (VI). Under the optimal concentration and room temperature, the fluorescence of the detection platform declined with increasing incubation time (0–7 min) and leveled off after 4 min. The reason may be that N-CDs and Cr (VI) are able to conduct completely by the IFE effect at 4 min. Therefore, 4 min was the ideal incubation duration. In all subsequent investigations for the detection of Cr (VI), N-CDs: 0.3 mg mL−1, incubation temperature: room temperature, incubation time: 4 min were used as the optimal conditions. The above parameters were tested for three times independently under each variable, and the mean values were then computed.

An excellent spectral overlap between the fluorophore's excitation spectrum and the absorber's absorption spectrum is necessary for the generation of IFE [42]. Therefore, the selection of suitable absorbers and fluorophores for IFE-based fluorescent chemo sensors is very important. UV–Vis absorption spectrum of K2Cr2O7 (Cr6+) overlaps quite precisely with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs, which possesses the conditions for the occurrence of the IFE effect, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. As a result, a portion excitation light of the N-CDs is absorbed by Cr (VI), and result in the fluorescence quenching. For the sake of building a nano-detection platform with high responsiveness to Cr (VI), positive charges of N-CDs were synthesized, and –NH2 groups in p-phenylenediamine are the source of positive charge, which are present as –NH3 + during the preparation of CDs [43]. Moreover, the zeta potential of N-CDs changed from 18.5 mV to 11.2 mV (Fig. 6b) in aqueous solution after adding Cr (VI). The reason is that Cr6+ exists as both HCrO4 − and CrO4 2− with negative charges in the pH = 6, which causes the integration between N-CDs and Cr6+ by the electrostatic attraction and supplies the chance for IFE [1,42]. The two types of fluorescence quenching, dynamic and static, are related to the variation of fluorescence lifetime. To verify the quenching and fluorescence recovery of N-CDs by Cr (VI) and AA, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CDs with and without Cr (VI) as well as the coexistence of Cr (VI) and AA were measured and analyzed by the nonlinear least squares method. And Fig. 6c showed that the average fluorescence lifetime values of the N-CDs, N-CDs + Cr (VI) and N-CDs + Cr (VI) + AA were 6.151 ns, 6.158 and 6.156 ns, respectively. And the fluorescence lifetimes were basically unchanged, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Cr (VI) and the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs@Cr (VI) by AA were static effects.

Different pH may be important for the sensitivity of detection platform, so the sensitivity of the assay platform for the detection of Cr (VI) under neutral (pH = 7.4, PBS buffer) and alkaline (pH = 9, Glycine-NaOH buffer) conditions was investigated (Fig. S6). Under neutral conditions (Fig. S6a), the quenching efficiency of N-CDs was only 77.3 % at a Cr (VI) concentration of 100 μM. Under alkaline conditions (Fig. S6b), the fluorescence quenching efficiency was 68.55 % at a concentration of 100 μM. Under acidic conditions (pH = 6), the fluorescence quenching efficiency was as high as 93.2 % at a Cr (VI) concentration of 100 μM (Fig. 7 a). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of N-CDs was highest under acidic conditions. In order to obtain greater fluorescence quenching and higher detection sensitivity, we used pH = 6 as the detection environment for Cr (VI) in the subsequent experiments. We explored the fluorescence changes of N-CDs in the presence of Cr (VI) at different concentrations under the above optimal conditions (N-CDs: 0.3 mg mL−1, PBS: pH = 6, 0.01 M, incubation temperature: room temperature, incubation time: 4 min). Fig. 7a shows the fluorescence of N-CDs was gradually quenched as the Cr (VI) concentration rose from 0 to 100 μM. When the Cr (VI) concentration reaches 90 μM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency can reach 91.05 %, which is a high quenching value [44]. Afterwards, the trend of fluorescence reduction gradually slowed down. It reached 93.2 % at the concentration of 100 μM, which was a very satisfactory result in comparison to other “turn-off-on” fluorescence platforms [19,22]. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs displayed a great linear relationship with the Cr (VI) concentration (0.01–40 μM). The linear correlation formula (eq (1)) as follows:
(eq 1)

The following formula (eq (2)) is used to compute LOD:

The calculated value of LOD was 0.0303 μM. This is the lower value documented thus far. The detection concentration of Cr (VI) was between 0.01 and 40 μM. As can be observed in the inset of Fig. 7b, the system solution gradually darkens with increasing Cr (VI) content under daylight (Fig. 7c) and the fluorescence is gradually quenched under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7d). The results of the experiments demonstrate that our building nanoplatform has outstanding Cr (VI) detection capabilities in comparison to those in other reports shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of fluorescence platform and detection for AA
ce:section.ce:para:
Under relatively mild circumstances (acidic or weakly basic solutions, room temperature), AA can convert Cr (VI) to Cr (III), then the pollution was declined. This fully reflects the “green” utilization of wastewater, reducing the toxicity and increasing the nutrient level of water bodies, and can be applied to daily life. At the same time, the fluorescence of N-CDs was restored due to the IFE effect between Cr (VI) and N-CDs was interrupted in presence of AA. So the N-CDs@Cr (VI) system can be taken as a “turn off-on” fluorescent sensor for testing AA. At Cr (VI) concentration of 90 μM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency could be as high as 91.05 %, which is a high quenching efficiency and can be a good concentration choice for the “turn-off-on” detection of AA. Therefore, an optimized fluorescence detection platform of N-CDs@Cr (VI), which were used to detect AA (CN-CDs = 0.3 mg mL−1, CCr (VI) = 90 μM) was constructed. Other test conditions were consistent with the above detection Cr (VI). Under room temperature condition, the optimized parameters of incubation duration were assessed via fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF) (ΔF=F
0 –F, F and F
0 denote the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@Cr (VI) at 510 nm with and without AA). The optimal incubation time was explored by adding 100 μM of AA to 2 mL of N-CDs@Cr (VI) at room temperature. According to Fig. S7, as incubation time increased, the N-CDs@Cr (VI) platform fluorescence was gradually recovered, and the fluorescence intensity reaches a peak at 3.5 min, indicating that AA entirely converted Cr (VI) to Cr (III). To eliminate interference factors, we chose 4 min as the optimal incubation time.

The sensitivity of N-CDs@Cr (VI) fluorescence detection platform for AA was investigated under optimal circumstances. The fluorescence of the N-CD@Cr (VI) platform was steadily enhanced as AA concentration rose from 0 to 600 μM in Fig. 7e. By collecting the changes of fluorescence intensity at 510 nm with increasing AA concentration (inset of Fig. 7e), we could find that the fluorescence intensity gradually increased and leveled off after the AA concentration reaches 100 μM. The fluorescence recovery efficiency could reach 81.16 % with AA concentration rising to 600 μM, which is a very impressive figure in a fluorescent “turn off-on” system [17]. The fluorescence intensity of the detection platform to the concentration variation of AA was linearly fitted and showed in Fig. 7f. In the range of 0.1–100 μM, fluorescence intensity and AA concentration displayed a strong linear correlation. The linear correlation formula (eq (3)) as follows:
(eq 3)

F 0
= 7.6268 X + 83.471
(
R 2
= 0.9942
)

F
0
and F is fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs@Cr (VI) without and with different concentrations of AA at 510 nm. X is the addition concentration of AA. R
2 is linear correlation coefficient.

LOD is determined using the equation (eq (2)). The obtained LOD is 0.072 μM, which is the lower value documented thus far. The range of AA's detection concentration was between 0.1 and 100 μM. From the inset of Fig. 7f. With AA concentration rising, the system solution fades under daylight (Fig. 7g) and the fluorescence is recovered under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 7h). Therefore, AA could be detected utilizing N-CDs@Cr (VI) as highly sensitive fluorescence platform. Comparison of the linear range and detection limits of the developed platforms with those previously reported (Table 1) indicates that our method is comparable or superior to them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity during the detection of Cr (VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the specificity of the N-CDs and N-CDs@Cr (VI) fluorescence detection platform for Cr (VI) and AA, respectively, we selected different interfering substances (Fig. 8 ) for detection experiments. The fluorescence of N-CDs at excited wavelength of 370 nm was investigated when different metallic and non-metallic interfering ions are present (Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Na+, Ni3+, Na+, Pb2+, Zn2+, V5+, Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr6+, 90 μM). As can be seen in Fig. 8a, when interference ions are present, the detection platform's fluorescence intensity is a certain degree of reduction after adding Fe2+ (19.15 %), Fe3+ (34 %) and Cu2+ (26.48 %), but only Cr6+ (91.05 %) induced a discernible decline. In the following, the causes of N-CDs fluorescence quenching by Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ were explored and analyzed by fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the three ion solutions (Fig. S8). The results demonstrated that UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ overlap less with fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs in comparison to that of Cr6+, so the IFE effect between metal ions and N-CD is weak, resulting in a slight fluorescence quenching [16,19,49]. Since Cr6+ is mainly present in the industrial effluent of leather manufacturing, while the content of Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ is negligible, so these three interfering ions do not affect our detection process. Taking together, this makes N-CDs be a highly selective fluorescence platform for the detection of Cr (VI).

To avoid false positives during the assay, the specific detection of ascorbic acid (AA) is very essential to the fluorescence detection platform N-CDs@Cr (VI). Therefore, different interfering substances, different types of amino acids contained in fruits (Fig. 8b) are selected such as: l-alanine (L-Ala), l-phenylalanine (L-Phe), glutamic acid (Glu), leucine (Leu), l-tryptophan (L-Trp), serine methyl ester hydrochloride (Ser), l-norvaline (L-Cbz), l-histidine (L-His), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), uric acid (UA), 600 μM. It was found that the fluorescence intensity in presence of AA was far superior to those after adding other substances. Though the fluorescence of the detection system can recover to 21.9 % after the addition of citric acid, this is lower in relative to that of AA (81.16 %), so it does not affect the detection of AA. It was demonstrated in several experiments that both the detection of Cr (VI) by N-CDs and the detection of AA by the N-CDs@Cr (VI) platform have excellent specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr (VI)and AA detection in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: When the fluorescence detection platform constructed is applied to the actual environment, it has more research significance. Therefore, N-CDs was utilized for measuring the Cr (VI) concentration in real river and lake water samples. Complete the detailed operations as described in section 2.4. Added different doses of Cr (VI) and mixed it with N-CDs, and then fixed the volume to 2 mL with PBS buffer (N-CDs: 0.3 mg mL−1). The fluorescence spectra (Ex: 370 nm, Em: 510 nm) were recorded after incubating for 4 min in the dark at room temperature (25 °C). Table 2 presents the outcomes. The recoveries of Cr (VI) reached 92.7 %∼114.1 % in the lake water and 105.71 %∼107.71 % in river water, the relative standard deviations (RSD) are 0.9 %∼2.42 % and 2.19 %∼3.03 %, respectively. These showed that N-CDs can be utilized as fluorescent probe to detect Cr (VI) in chromium contaminated groundwater. Furthermore, the content of AA in actual fruit samples was determined by N-CDs@Cr (VI) method. The orange juices were diluted 50-fold in ultrapure water to the appropriate concentration within the assay range, and then added different concentrations of AA [49]. The detailed operations were completed as described in section 2.4. Table 2 presents the results. It can be seen that the contents of AA are different in pure Daming Lake water and pure Yufu River water. As we all know, AA is generally exudated from plant roots, aquatic macrophytes and phytoplankton, and may also come from the decomposition of organic matter (e.g., apomictic material, dead roots, algal cells) and be present in the soil or aqueous phases [50,51], and these are different in Yufu River water and Daming Lake water. So the detection results are different. In addition, the recovery rate of AA in the actual samples was able to reach 91.71 %∼104.68 % in lake and 92.85 %∼103.54 % in river water containing Cr (VI), respectively, with the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 3.24 %∼3.83 % and 1.45 %∼3.17 %. For examining the feasibility of the method we have established, the detection of Cr (VI) and AA by the fluorescence detection platform was compared with the standard detecting method for Cr (VI) (ICP method) and for AA (HPLC method), and the results are supplemented in Table 2. In the standard method, the recovery of Cr (VI) (ICP method) in real samples ranged from 93.36 % to 104.48 % (lake water) and 96.15 %–102.04 % (river water), with the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.13 %–2.14 % and 0.56 %–1.83 %, respectively. The recovery of AA (HPLC method) ranged from 95.42 % to 101.36 % (lake water) and 95.43 %–98.64 % (river water) with the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 0.75%–2.19 % and 1.14%–3.24 %, respectively. It can be seen that the values of Cr (VI) and AA detected by this fluorescence detection platform are comparable to those of the standard method, which indicates that the detection of Cr (VI) and AA by this fluorescence detection platform is highly accurate. A series of spiked experiments confirmed the promising application of the N-CDs@Cr (VI) platform for detecting AA quantificationally in fresh fruit samples in actual water samples containing Cr (VI). This method can remove Cr (VI) to improve water quality and detection AA and be expected to be applied in real life.

In summary, just like our hypothesis, yellow-green fluorescence emitting N-CDs with a big Stokes shift (140 nm) were produced using p-phenylenediamine doping cellulose acetate by one-step hydrothermal method. The preparation study showed N-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties under the reaction time of 12 h at 180 °C. Moreover, the excitation spectrum of N-CDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cr (VI) overlaps well, and Cr (VI) can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs (93.2 %) through the IFE effect. So a nanometer fluorescence detection platform was established to quantitatively detect Cr (VI). The fluorescence platform of N-CDs has a detection range between 0.01 and 40 μM with a lower LOD of 0.0303 μM, and has been successfully applied to the determination of Cr (VI) in actual water samples. Furthermore, Cr (VI) can be converted to Cr (III) in presence of AA, thus the IFE effect was interrupted and fluorescence was restored (81.16 %), so a “turn-off-on” fluorescence detection platform of N-CDs@Cr (VI) for AA was constructed and optimized (CN-CDs = 0.3 mg mL−1, CCr (VI) = 90 μM; incubation time: 4 min; temperature: room temperature; pH = 6 in PBS). The platform has a lower LOD (0.072 μM) and a broad detection range between 0.1 and 100 μM for AA. Lastly, the actual water samples containing Cr (VI) combined with N-CDs, and the actual orange samples was detected by the platform of N-CDs@Cr (VI). and the results for the spiked recovery experiments in the actual samples are satisfactory, which indicated that the fluorescence platforms for Cr (VI) and AA have a broad application prospect in environmental and bio-related fields.
